content.full_text:
A-84:260

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 7007 • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012

October 12, 1984

Mr. Alan Skobin
Calphin Ford
15505 Roscoe Boulevard
Sepulveda, California 91343

Dear Mr. Skobin:

Pursuant to your request, this letter will confirm my advice to you on
October 1, 1984.

The facts presented are as follows:

1. A company which sells potholders to political campaigns also knows a
campaign consultant in Sacramento who periodically refers orders for potholders
to this company.

2. A campaign committee has hired this campaign consultant and orders
political potholders from the company recommended by the consultant.

3. The campaign committee pays the potholder company directly for their
order.

4. A commission, or "finders fee" is paid by the potholder company to the
consultant unbeknownst to the campaign committee.

5. This commission is paid to the campaign consultant each time he refers
a political order to their company and is based on the amount received and a
prenegotiated volume price.

The question you asked was, whether or not the paid commission had to be
reported as an "expenditure" by the potholder company or by the consultant;
and if it was to be reported, how is it to be reported and by whom?

After discussing the facts with Jeanne Pritchard of our Sacramento office
I stated that the commission, or finders fee paid by the potholder company to
the campaign consultant is not a reportable expenditure as defined by the Political
Reform Act. Government Code Section 82025, in part, defines an expenditure as a
payment that is made for "political purposes". "Political purposes" is further
defined in 2 Cal. Admin. 18225 as being for "the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election
of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure."

SACRAMENTO (916) 440 • LOS ANGELES (213) 620-5196

Mr. Alan Skobin
Page Two
October 12, 1984


     Since the campaign consultant and the podholder company have an agreement
that a commission, based on a prenegotiated volume price, will be paid for each
order placed, the commission staff views this to be a normal working business
agreement between the consultant and the podholder company and is not an expendi-
ture for political purposes.

     Therefore, the podholder company is not required to file any campaign state-
ment reporting the payment of the commission to the consultant and the consultant
is not required to disclose the receipt of the commission.

     I have enclosed copies of 2 Cal. Admin. 18225 and 18431 for your information.
Please do not hesitate to contact me if I can be of any further assistance.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        HELEN P. ARRIOLA
                                        Political Reform Consultant

HPA:cv
Enclosure

sections.question: Whether or not the paid commission had to be reported as an "expenditure" by the potholder company or by the consultant; and if it was to be reported, how is it to be reported and by whom?
sections.conclusion: Since the campaign consultant and the potholder company have an agreement that a commission, based on a prenegotiated volume price, will be paid for each order placed, the commission staff views this to be a normal working business agreement between the consultant and the potholder company and is not an expenditure for political purposes. Therefore, the potholder company is not required to file any campaign statement reporting the payment of the commission to the consultant and the consultant is not required to disclose the receipt of the commission.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a campaign consultant disclose commissions received from a vendor for referring political orders, and if so, how should such payments be reported as expenditures under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the commission is not a reportable expenditure under the Political Reform Act because it represents a normal business agreement rather than a payment made for political purposes. Neither the potholder company nor the consultant is required to disclose or report the commission.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a confirmation letter dated October 12, 1984, following a phone consultation on October 1, 1984. Clear Q&C structure with explicit question and conclusion sections. Letter references Government Code Section 82025 and California Administrative Code sections 18225 and 18431.

QUESTION: Whether or not the paid commission had to be reported as an "expenditure" by the potholder company or by the consultant; and if it was to be reported, how is it to be reported and by whom?

CONCLUSION: Since the campaign consultant and the potholder company have an agreement that a commission, based on a prenegotiated volume price, will be paid for each order placed, the commission staff views this to be a normal working business agreement between the consultant and the potholder company and is not an expenditure for political purposes. Therefore, the potholder company is not required to file any campaign statement reporting the payment of the commission to the consultant and the consultant is not required to disclose the receipt of the commission.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirmed advice that a commission or finder's fee paid by a potholder vendor to a campaign consultant for referring political orders is not a reportable expenditure under California's Political Reform Act, as it constitutes a normal business arrangement rather than a payment for political purposes.
